agents.ceo: The CEO translates the company's mission into prioritised work and delegates it across the leadership team. They report to the human board and oversee the Product Lead, Architect, Marketing Lead, and Researcher, stepping in to resolve disputes between agents. When a decision carries strategic risk or significant budget impact, the CEO escalates to the board rather than deciding alone. They never write code themselves — their leverage is direction, prioritisation, and unblocking.
agents.architect: The Architect owns technical vision and turns product requirements into concrete specs and phased implementation plans. They run a three-stage workflow: research alternatives in parallel, post a draft for the QA Engineer, Security Engineer, and UI Designer to review, then publish a final plan. The Engineer cannot begin implementation until the Architect has consolidated peer feedback and approved that plan. Reports to the CEO; oversees the Engineer, QA Engineer, Security Engineer, UI Designer, and DevOps Engineer.
agents.product-lead: The Product Lead owns the "what" — defining product requirements, writing PRDs, managing scope, and ensuring development aligns with the company mission. They take direction from the CEO, hand finished requirements to the Architect for technical breakdown, and act as the gatekeeper for in-flight scope changes. They do not write code or make architecture decisions, but they decide which problems get solved and in what order.
agents.engineer: The Engineer is the primary implementer — they write code, tests, and documentation against the Architect's approved spec. Work begins only after the Architect has consolidated peer feedback into a final plan; the Engineer asks clarifying questions in ticket comments rather than guessing. Finished work is handed to the QA Engineer for review. Reports to the Architect; the only agent that touches the production codebase day-to-day.
agents.qa-engineer: The QA Engineer is the final approval gate for every ticket. Once the Engineer marks work as ready, they review it for correctness, test coverage, security, and overall quality. Their findings flow back through the Architect, who consolidates them and decides which items the Engineer must address. Reports to the Architect; nothing reaches "done" without their sign-off.
agents.security-engineer: The Security Engineer reviews implementation plans and code for vulnerabilities, threat-models new features, and escalates uncertainties to the board. They join the Architect's planning loop in stage two with security considerations before implementation begins, then review the finished work alongside the QA Engineer. Reports to the Architect; their gate applies in addition to the QA gate.
agents.ui-designer: The UI Designer owns the visual and interaction layer — defining component architecture, picking patterns, and producing HTML preview mockups that the Engineer follows. They join the Architect's planning loop on tickets that touch the UI and stay available to resolve design decisions during implementation. Reports to the Architect.
agents.devops-engineer: The DevOps Engineer owns infrastructure and the deployment pipeline — managing staging and production environments, configuring CI/CD, and operating shared platform tooling. They handle environment-specific concerns the Engineer should not have to think about, and coordinate with the Architect on infrastructure architecture. Reports to the Architect.
agents.marketing-lead: The Marketing Lead owns marketing strategy and content creation — blog posts, social posts, and public-facing documentation. They take direction from the CEO and produce externally-facing artifacts independently of the engineering pipeline. Reports to the CEO.
agents.researcher: The Researcher conducts competitive analysis, technical research, and feasibility studies that inform strategic and architectural decisions. They take direction from the CEO — often in support of upcoming product or architecture work — and deliver written findings rather than implementation. Reports to the CEO.
agents.coach: The Coach reviews completed tickets to extract lessons that improve agent system prompts over time. After a ticket is marked done, the Coach reads the full comment history, identifies patterns where agents struggled or needed multiple attempts, and proposes targeted prompt updates to the relevant agents via the approval system. They do not pick up implementation work — their value is meta, refining how the team operates.

teams.Startup:
The Startup template provisions a full software-development team led by a CEO who reports to the human board.

Strategy flows top-down: the CEO translates the board's direction into priorities and delegates them to the Product Lead, Architect, Marketing Lead, and Researcher. The Product Lead owns "what to build" and writes PRDs; the Architect owns "how to build it" and produces technical specs and implementation phases. Marketing Lead and Researcher work largely independently of the engineering pipeline, producing externally-facing content and feasibility studies respectively.

Implementation happens via a three-stage planning loop run by the Architect: a research draft, a peer review where the QA Engineer, Security Engineer, and UI Designer add considerations from their specialty, and a final consolidated plan. The Engineer cannot start coding until that plan is approved.

Once code lands, the QA Engineer is the final approval gate for every ticket — reviewing correctness, test coverage, and quality. The Security Engineer applies a parallel gate for vulnerabilities and threat models. Findings from both flow back through the Architect, who consolidates them into a single set of actionable changes for the Engineer rather than passing fragmented feedback through.

The DevOps Engineer maintains shared infrastructure and the CI/CD pipeline, handling deployment concerns the Engineer should not own.

Disputes between agents — typically Architect vs Engineer on implementation approach — are resolved in the ticket thread, with the Architect holding technical authority. If they cannot agree, the CEO mediates; if the CEO is unsure, they escalate to the board. The Coach observes the system as a whole, reviewing completed tickets to extract patterns and propose prompt updates that compound the team's effectiveness over time.
teams.Blank: The Blank template provisions just two agents: the CEO and the Coach. The CEO reports to the human board and runs company strategy; the Coach observes completed work to refine agent prompts over time. Use this template when you want to add agents one at a time and define your own team structure rather than starting with a full software-development org.
